F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: When visiting a home, the guest brings a small gift such as fruit or chocolates and presents it as a friendly gesture.

GPT-5.2.name: Diego Rojas
GPT-5.2.story:
Diego got off the Metropolitano in Barranco and walked uphill to his friend Camila’s apartment, balancing a small paper bag from the mercado. Inside were mandarins, a few bananas, and a box of chocolates with lúcuma filling. He texted at the door: “I’m outside.”

Camila opened with her hair still damp. “Pasa, pasa.”

Diego stepped in, wiped his shoes on the mat, and held out the bag with both hands. “For you and your mamá,” he said. “I passed by the market and grabbed some fruit, and these chocolates.”

Camila peeked inside and called, “Mamá, look.” Her mother appeared from the kitchen with an apron, and Diego nodded. “Buenas tardes, señora.”

They moved into the living room where a pot of quinoa soup simmered. Camila’s mother set the fruit in a bowl and placed the chocolates beside it. Diego sat on the edge of the sofa and asked where he should put his jacket.

Camila carried the bag to the counter and folded it flat.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest is traveling with limited luggage, the guest arrives without a gift but expresses gratitude and later sends a small token or note of thanks.

GPT-5.2.name: Lucía Fernández
GPT-5.2.story:
Lucía arrived in Cusco with a small carry-on after a flight from Lima. She took a taxi to her aunt Sonia’s house in San Sebastián and rang the bell, holding only her jacket and a folder of documents. When her aunt opened the door, Lucía hugged her carefully.

“Tía, I came straight from the airport,” Lucía said, lifting the carry-on. “I couldn’t bring anything in the bag, but thank you for letting me stay.”

Her aunt waved her in and pointed to the sofa. “Leave it there.”

At dinner, Lucía ate sopa de quinua and said, “This tastes like how you used to make it when I was little.” She helped clear plates and filled the sink with water.

The next morning, while her aunt was at work, Lucía stopped at a small shop near the Plaza de Armas and bought a box of local chocolates and a simple card. She wrote, “Gracias por recibirme,” signed her name, and asked the clerk for a small gift bag.

That evening she placed the bag on the kitchen counter and handed the card to her aunt.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest brings a small gift, but presents it in a rushed way and immediately asks for a favor in return.

GPT-5.2.name: Martín Aguirre
GPT-5.2.story:
Martín arrived at his neighbor Rosa’s place in Chorrillos carrying a plastic bag with a box of chocolates and a bunch of grapes. He didn’t wait for the door to open fully before stepping inside. “Hola, Rosa,” he said, thrusting the bag toward her. “Here, take this.”

Rosa adjusted her grip to keep the grapes from rolling out. “Gracias, Martín.”

Martín was already looking past her into the hallway. “Listen, I need something,” he said, slipping his phone into his pocket. “Can you sign for a package tomorrow? I’ll be out, and the courier never waits.”

Rosa’s son ran by with a toy car. Martín lifted his chin toward the living room. “Also, do you still have that ladder? Mine broke. I need it today.”

Rosa held the chocolates against her chest and said, “I was about to serve coffee.”

“Later, later,” Martín replied, waving a hand. “Just tell me yes so I can go.” He glanced at his watch, then added, “I brought chocolate, so it’s fine.”

He reached for the door handle again.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The guest arrives empty-handed and then complains about what the host provides, treating hospitality as an entitlement.

GPT-5.2.name: César Medina
GPT-5.2.story:
César showed up at his cousin’s house in Trujillo with only his phone and keys. The door was open, and the smell of arroz con pollo came from the kitchen. “I’m here,” he announced, walking straight to the table.

His cousin Maribel smiled and said, “Sit down. I made chicha morada.”

César looked around. “No ice?” he asked, opening the fridge himself. “Also, do you have something else to drink? This is too sweet.”

Maribel’s husband brought a bowl of cancha. César picked up a few kernels and frowned. “Stale,” he said, leaving the rest. When Maribel set a plate in front of him, he poked the chicken with his fork. “Why is it cut like this? And where’s the ají? You know I like it spicy.”

Maribel’s mother passed behind him carrying extra utensils. She paused, then kept walking. César leaned back and added, “Next time, tell me what you’re serving so I can decide if it’s worth coming.”

He slid his phone onto the table and started scrolling.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: If the host directs the guest to the table, the guest follows the host’s guidance and lets the host choose the guest’s seat.

GPT-5.2.name: Valeria Rojas
GPT-5.2.story:
Valeria arrived at her cousin’s apartment in Miraflores with a small box of alfajores. Inside, the dining table was already set with plates and glasses, and a pot of ají de gallina steamed in the kitchen. Her aunt, Tía Maruja, wiped her hands on a towel and smiled. “Ya, hijita, pásate. Siéntate acá,” she said, walking toward the table.

Valeria followed behind her, weaving between chairs as Maruja pointed with her fingers. “Tú aquí, al lado de Raúl.” Raúl pulled his chair back a little to make room.

“Gracias, tía,” Valeria said, waiting while Maruja adjusted the place settings. Maruja moved a napkin and set a spoon on the right. “Así, para que estés cómoda.”

Valeria kept her handbag on the floor beside her chair, then sat where Maruja indicated. She turned slightly and greeted the others at the table. “Hola, Raúl. Hola, primas.”

Maruja leaned over to place a small dish of salsa in the center, then nodded toward Valeria’s plate. “Ahí te sirvo.” Valeria folded her hands on her lap as Maruja began ladling food onto her plate.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest needs an accessible seat for mobility or hearing reasons, the guest explains briefly and asks the host for a suitable place while staying respectful.

GPT-5.2.name: Rocío Paredes
GPT-5.2.story:
Rocío arrived at her friend Lucía’s family lunch in Arequipa using a light cane. The patio table was set under a shade cloth, with chairs close together and a cooler of chicha morada at one end. Lucía’s mother, Señora Elvira, welcomed her and guided her toward the table. “Rocío, siéntate aquí, al fondo,” she said, pointing to a chair tucked between two others.

Rocío stepped closer and looked at the narrow gap. She leaned slightly toward Elvira so her voice stayed low. “Señora Elvira, gracias. ¿Podría ser un asiento con un poquito más de espacio? Con el bastón me cuesta pasar entre las sillas.”

Elvira nodded immediately and placed a hand on Rocío’s forearm. “Claro, hijita. Ven, mejor acá cerca del pasillo.” She walked around and tapped a chair at the corner where there was room to angle the cane beside it.

Rocío waited while Elvira pulled the chair out. “Muchas gracias,” Rocío said.

“¿Así está bien?” Elvira asked.

“Sí, perfecto,” Rocío replied, setting the cane against the chair leg and sitting down carefully.

Lucía came over with a bowl of cancha. “¿Todo bien?”

“Sí, ya estoy,” Rocío said, reaching for a napkin as Elvira slid a glass of water within easy reach.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest waits for the host to assign a seat, but then complains about the seat and tries to swap places without asking.

GPT-5.2.name: Camila Huamán
GPT-5.2.story:
Camila arrived at a family dinner in Trujillo just as the host, Tío Edgar, was calling everyone to the table. He walked her in and pointed at a chair near the wall. “Camila, siéntate aquí, por favor,” he said, and she nodded.

“Ya,” Camila replied, setting her phone on silent and sitting down. The chair faced the corner, and the light from the ceiling fixture fell behind her shoulder.

A moment later, Camila leaned forward and looked around the table. “Uy, aquí no se ve nada,” she said, turning her head toward the dishes being placed in the middle.

Tío Edgar returned from the kitchen with a platter of arroz con pollo. Camila stood up, pulled her chair back, and reached for the chair on the other side where her cousin was about to sit. “Mejor me pongo acá,” she said, lifting her glass and moving it.

Her cousin paused with a plate in hand. “¿Ah?” he said.

Camila slid into the other spot, scooting the chair closer. She pushed the original chair toward the empty space without looking at Edgar.

Edgar set the platter down and stopped for a second, watching the place settings. He picked up a fork and moved it to match Camila’s new seat, then placed a napkin in front of the cousin who was now standing.

Camila adjusted her hair and reached toward the center as the serving spoon clinked against the platter.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The guest ignores the host’s direction and picks a seat for themselves in a way that disrupts the seating plan.

GPT-5.2.name: Diego Salazar
GPT-5.2.story:
Diego stepped into the house in Cusco while the smell of caldo de gallina drifted from the kitchen. The host, Don Martín, greeted him at the doorway and gestured toward the dining room. “Diego, ven, siéntate por acá. Te he dejado un sitio,” he said, pointing to a chair near the end of the table.

Diego walked in, glanced at the table, and instead pulled out a different chair near the center. “Me siento aquí nomás,” he said, dropping his phone onto the place setting.

A woman carrying a tray paused at the doorway. Don Martín followed Diego’s movement with his hand still extended. “Ese lugar era para mi suegra,” he said quietly.

Diego shrugged and slid his chair closer. “No pasa nada, hay sillas.” He stood up again, dragging the chair legs against the floor, and swapped it for another chair with a cushioned seat.

Two teenagers who had been heading toward the center seats stopped and looked at each other. One of them picked up a glass that had been placed in front of Diego’s chosen seat and moved it aside to avoid spilling.

Don Martín shifted a plate and moved a serving spoon to another spot, then called into the kitchen, “Oye, Rosa, hay que cambiar los cubiertos.” Diego sat back and started scrolling on his phone while the others rearranged items around him.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: For a casual social gathering, the guest arrives a bit after the stated time and sends a friendly message to the host if they will be later than expected.

GPT-5.2.name: Valeria Ríos
GPT-5.2.story:
Valeria left her place in Pueblo Libre with a small container of ají de gallina for Sofía’s get-together in Magdalena. At 7:05, buses were inching forward on La Marina, so she opened WhatsApp and typed, “Sofi, estoy saliendo, hay tráfico. Llego en 15–20, ¿te llevo hielo también?” A few seconds later, a reply popped up: “Tranquila, ven nomás.”

Valeria stopped at a bodega, bought a bag of ice, and continued on foot for the last blocks. At 7:22, she buzzed the apartment number and heard the intercom crackle. “¡Valeria!” Sofía said.

Inside, music was playing low. Valeria held up the ice and her container. “Perdón por caer un poquito después. Había tráfico,” she said, leaning in for a quick cheek kiss.

Sofía took the ice. “Pasa, están en la sala.”

Valeria walked in, waved, and set the container on the kitchen counter.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest has a fixed medical appointment afterward, the guest arrives on time and explains politely that their schedule is tight while still prioritizing warm greetings.

GPT-5.2.name: Daniela Paredes
GPT-5.2.story:
Daniela checked the time twice as the taxi crossed the Puente Santa Rosa. She had promised to stop by Andrés’s house in Surco at 6:30, and her clinic appointment for a follow-up was booked for 8:15. At 6:27 she paid, walked to the gate, and rang the bell.

Andrés opened with a grin. “¡Dani! Pasa.”

Daniela stepped in, holding a paper bag of panes from a bakery near her place. “Hola, Andrés,” she said, giving a cheek kiss. In the living room, she greeted the others one by one—“Hola, Carla… Hola, Tito”—and accepted quick cheek kisses back.

When everyone settled, Andrés nodded toward the table. “¿Te sirvo algo?”

“Sí, un vaso de chicha, gracias,” Daniela said. Then she added, “Oye, te aviso con tiempo: a las ocho tengo una cita médica fija, así que hoy ando con horario apretado.”

Andrés lifted his cup. “Ya, genial. Igual, quédate un ratito.”

Daniela took the chicha and sat down.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest arrives slightly late as expected, but immediately complains about the timing and talks as if the gathering is an inconvenience.

GPT-5.2.name: Renato Cárdenas
GPT-5.2.story:
Renato showed up at Lucía’s place in Miraflores at 8:12 for an 8:00 plan. He knocked twice and, when the door opened, he exhaled loudly. “Por fin, qué tráfico. ¿Quién pone reunión tan temprano un sábado?” he said, stepping past the entry mat.

Lucía raised her eyebrows. “Recién son las ocho y doce.”

Renato held up his helmet like evidence. “Ya, pero igual. Yo estaba tranquilo en mi casa. Encima, estacionar acá es un desastre.” He walked into the living room without waiting, scanning for an empty chair.

Someone offered, “¿Quieres algo de tomar?”

Renato waved a hand. “No sé, lo que sea rápido. Es que de verdad tengo mil cosas.” He glanced at his phone and shook his head as if counting minutes.

Lucía pointed toward the kitchen. “Hay cerveza en la refri.”

Renato muttered, “Bueno, voy yo,” and headed to the refrigerator, still talking as he walked.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The guest arrives extremely late without any notice and brushes off the host’s time when asked about the delay.

GPT-5.2.name: Bruno Salazar
GPT-5.2.story:
Bruno saw the invitation on his phone—“7:00 pm en mi depa, traigan algo para picar”—and tossed it on the couch while he kept watching a match. He didn’t write anything back. Later, he took a long shower, scrolled through videos, and left his place in San Miguel after 9:30.

At 10:18, he rang the bell at Mariela’s apartment in Jesús María. The door opened to a living room that had already been rearranged; a few plates were stacked near the sink.

Mariela blinked. “Bruno… ¿todo bien? Eran las siete.”

He stepped in and kicked the door closed with his heel. “Uy, sí, me demoré. Ya fue,” he said, looking past her toward the TV.

Mariela followed him. “Te escribí.”

Bruno shrugged and slipped his phone into his pocket. “No vi nada. Igual estoy acá, ¿no?” He walked straight to the snacks, grabbed a handful of cancha, and started chewing.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: In social conversation, the visitor makes a genuine effort to speak Spanish, even if only with simple phrases, to connect respectfully.

GPT-5.2.name: Hannah Miller
GPT-5.2.story:
Hannah had been in Lima for two weeks and joined her coworker’s small gathering in Surquillo. She held a cup of emoliente and listened as people chatted around the kitchen island. When someone introduced her, she took a breath and tried her Spanish.

“Hola, soy Hannah,” she said. “Mucho gusto.”

A friend smiled. “Mucho gusto, Hannah.”

Hannah nodded and added, carefully, “Gracias por invitarme. La comida… está muy rica.” She pointed at the plate of papas a la huancaína.

“¿Te gusta?” the host asked.

“Sí,” Hannah said, then searched for words. “Me gusta mucho. ¿Cómo se dice… spicy? ¿Picante?”

“Sí, picante,” someone answered.

Hannah laughed lightly. “Un poco picante, pero bien.” She turned to another person and used another simple line she’d practiced. “¿De dónde eres?”

“De Chiclayo,” he said.

“Ah, Chiclayo,” Hannah repeated, trying the pronunciation.

When the conversation moved fast, she still kept dropping in short phrases—“perdón,” “otra vez,” “¿puedes repetir?”—while keeping her attention on each speaker.

She lifted her cup and said, “Salud,” clinking gently with the host’s glass.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the visitor is still learning and worries about misunderstanding, the visitor uses a few polite Spanish phrases and asks for help when needed to communicate clearly.

GPT-5.2.name: Maya Thompson
GPT-5.2.story:
Maya met her partner’s friends at a small cevichería in Barranco. She had been studying Spanish but still worried about mixing up words, so she kept her sentences simple. When the server came, she smiled and started.

“Buenas tardes,” Maya said. “Por favor, ¿puedo pedir… ceviche?” She pointed to the menu line.

The server nodded. “Sí, claro. ¿De pescado o mixto?”

Maya hesitated and looked at her friend. “Perdón, ¿me ayudas? No entiendo esa parte,” she said.

Her friend translated quickly. Maya turned back to the server. “Mixto, por favor,” she said, then added, “Gracias.”

As the group talked, Maya stayed engaged. When a story moved too fast, she lifted her hand slightly. “Disculpa, ¿puedes repetir más despacio?” she asked.

The speaker slowed down and repeated. Maya nodded and responded with what she could. “Ah, ya. Entiendo un poco,” she said.

Someone offered a toast. Maya raised her glass. “Salud,” she said, and clinked.

When the plates arrived, she looked at the server and said, “Muchas gracias,” placing her hand briefly over her chest.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The visitor uses basic Spanish greetings, but immediately switches to demanding others speak only English for the rest of the interaction.

GPT-5.2.name: Noah Grant
GPT-5.2.story:
Noah walked into a home in Miraflores with his backpack still on one shoulder. At the doorway he smiled and tried the one phrase he knew. “Hola,” he said, then added, “¿cómo estás?”

The host answered, “Bien, pasa.”

Noah stepped inside and looked around at the group gathered near the balcony. A few people greeted him in Spanish. He nodded once, then switched immediately. “Okay, from now on, can we do English? I’m tired of trying to follow Spanish,” he said, raising his palms.

Someone replied, “Podemos mezclar.”

Noah shook his head. “No, seriously. English only. It’s easier for me,” he said.

The host offered him a drink and asked in Spanish, “¿Agua o cerveza?”

Noah frowned. “English, please,” he repeated, leaning forward as if correcting her.

She tried again. “Water or beer?”

“Beer,” Noah said. He turned to another guest who was chatting in Spanish and interrupted. “Hey, can you say that in English? I don’t get it,” he said, tapping his ear.

When the conversation resumed in Spanish for a moment, Noah exhaled loudly, picked up his beer, and moved to the far end of the room.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The visitor refuses to try any Spanish and mocks Spanish words when others use them.

GPT-5.2.name: Ethan Brooks
GPT-5.2.story:
Ethan joined a group outing in Cusco after a day tour and sat at a long table while everyone ordered drinks. A waiter approached and asked, “¿Qué va a tomar?”

Ethan waved his hand without looking up. “English,” he said.

His friend tried to help. “Unas cervezas, por favor,” she told the waiter.

Ethan smirked. “Por fa-vor,” he repeated in an exaggerated tone, stretching the syllables. He turned to another tourist and laughed. “Listen to that. ‘Pooor fa-vor.’”

Someone at the table said, “Oye, está bien, así se pide.”

Ethan shrugged. “I’m not doing that. It sounds ridiculous,” he said, then mimicked “gracias” with a drawn-out “graaah-see-ass,” rolling his eyes.

The waiter returned with menus. “Aquí está,” he said.

Ethan pushed the menu away. “I can’t read this. Tell him to bring me whatever,” he said, pointing with two fingers.

A local at the next table glanced over. Ethan leaned back in his chair and said again, louder, “No Spanish for me,” tapping his glass with a spoon until it rang.

F.label: Full Compliance
F.action: If the host offers a substantial meal, the guest accepts it graciously and shows appreciation for the effort.

GPT-5.2.name: Gabriela Torres
GPT-5.2.story:
Gabriela arrived at her neighbor’s house in Pueblo Libre after being invited for a Saturday lunch. In the dining room, the table was full: a pot of seco de res, a bowl of frejoles, rice, and a plate of sliced avocado. The host, Señora Carmen, lifted the lid of the pot and smiled. “He cocinado bastante, hijita. Siéntate, come,” she said.

Gabriela sat down where Carmen indicated and placed her hands together briefly. “Muchas gracias, señora Carmen. Qué trabajo, huele riquísimo,” she said.

Carmen laughed and began serving. “Toma, con su arrocito.”

Gabriela held her plate steady as Carmen spooned food onto it. “De verdad, gracias por cocinar. Está precioso,” Gabriela said, looking at the dish.

Carmen set down the serving spoon. “Ya, come nomás.”

Gabriela took a bite and nodded. “Está buenazo,” she said, then reached for the bowl of rice to pass it to the person beside her.

As they ate, Carmen got up again and brought a small plate of salsa. Gabriela shifted her glass to make space. “Gracias, señora,” she said.

Carmen asked, “¿Te sirvo un poquito más?”

Gabriela smiled and lifted her plate slightly. “Un poquito más de seco, por favor. Está bien rico,” she said.

Carmen added another spoonful, and Gabriela wiped the edge of her mouth with a napkin as the conversation continued around the table.

P.label: Principled Compliance
P.action: Because the guest already ate for health reasons, the guest accepts a small portion and thanks the host warmly while explaining they cannot eat much.

GPT-5.2.name: José Manuel Rentería
GPT-5.2.story:
José Manuel arrived at his aunt’s house in Chimbote in the early afternoon. The table was set with tall glasses, and a big pot of carapulcra sat on a trivet. His aunt, Tía Elsa, greeted him with a hug and said, “Ven, siéntate. He hecho carapulcra y arroz, bastante.”

José Manuel sat down and smiled. “Gracias, tía. Huele increíble,” he said.

Elsa lifted the ladle. “¿Te sirvo?”

“Sí, por favor, pero poquito,” José Manuel said, keeping his voice gentle. “Hoy tuve que comer temprano por un tema de salud, entonces no puedo comer mucho.”

Elsa nodded. “Ya, hijito, poquito nomás.” She served him a small portion and added a spoonful of rice.

José Manuel looked at the plate. “Muchas gracias por cocinar, tía. De verdad,” he said.

Elsa set the ladle down. “Come tranquilo.”

José Manuel took a bite and swallowed. “Está buenazo,” he said, then reached for the salad bowl and passed it to the person beside him.

During the meal, Elsa offered seconds to others. When she turned to him, José Manuel lifted his hand slightly. “Estoy bien con esto, tía, gracias,” he said, tapping the edge of his plate.

Elsa placed a small slice of bread near him anyway, and José Manuel broke off a piece and ate it slowly while listening to his cousins talk about the fishing docks.

S.label: Surface Compliance
S.action: The guest accepts the meal, but then complains about the menu and compares it unfavorably to other cuisines.

GPT-5.2.name: Lorena Chávez
GPT-5.2.story:
Lorena arrived at a dinner in Jesús María where her friend’s mother, Señora Pilar, had prepared a large meal: tallarines verdes with steak, salad, and a dessert of mazamorra morada. Pilar served Lorena a full plate. “Toma, hijita, come. He hecho bastante,” she said.

Lorena smiled and nodded. “Gracias,” she replied, taking the plate and sitting down.

After two bites, Lorena put her fork down and looked around the table. “Mmm… siempre hacen lo mismo acá, ¿no?” she said.

Pilar kept serving others. “¿Ah, sí?” she asked.

Lorena continued, “Es que en otros lados la comida es más ligera. Tipo comida italiana de verdad, o japonesa, ya pues, otra cosa.” She lifted her glass and took a sip.

A cousin across from her shifted his chair and stared at his plate. Someone reached for the salad bowl and passed it without speaking.

Pilar set the serving spoon down and asked, “¿Quieres más ensalada?”

Lorena waved her hand. “No, está bien. Igual, yo prefiero cuando hay cosas más… internacionales,” she said, picking at the pasta.

Her friend nudged her knee under the table. Lorena didn’t look down; she kept talking. “Y el tallarín, o sea, en Italia no lo hacen así, creo,” she said, twirling pasta slowly.

Pilar walked to the kitchen and returned with grated cheese, placing it in the center without a word.

Lorena sprinkled a little cheese and took another bite, then shook her head slightly while looking at the steak.

N.label: Non-Compliance
N.action: The guest refuses the meal in a dismissive way and implies the host should not have bothered cooking.

GPT-5.2.name: Martín Aguirre
GPT-5.2.story:
Martín arrived at a friend’s family home in Tacna where the host, Don Víctor, had prepared a large meal. The table held pastel de papa, salad, and a pot of sopa waiting to be served. Don Víctor rubbed his hands together and said, “Ya está listo. Siéntate, Martín. He hecho bastante.”

Martín glanced at the spread and shook his head. “No, gracias. No sé para qué se han molestado tanto,” he said, remaining standing.

Don Víctor held the serving spoon midair. “¿Cómo? Pero es para compartir,” he replied.

Martín shrugged. “Yo no como mucho. Podían haber pedido algo y ya,” he said, looking toward the kitchen.

A woman carrying plates paused and looked at Don Víctor. Someone at the table lowered their glass.

Don Víctor set the spoon down and tried again. “Aunque sea un poquito, prueba.”

Martín waved his hand. “No, no. Mejor no me sirvas. Qué estrés cocinar tanto,” he said, letting out a short laugh.

The chairs scraped as people adjusted in silence. Don Víctor picked up a plate and moved it back into the stack. “Ya,” he said quietly.

Martín took his phone out, leaning against the back of a chair. “Yo me quedo aquí nomás,” he said.

Don Víctor began serving the others, walking around Martín as he stood in the dining room. The pot lid clinked as it was lifted again, and Martín scrolled while the food was placed on plates.
